retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Corticobulbar Tract -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Corticobulbar tract carries upper motor neuron input to motor nuclei of trigeminal, facial, glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory, and hypoglossal nerves. The motor component of trigeminal nerves supplies muscles of mastication. The facial nerve supplies the muscles of facial expression. Glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory nerve innervates muscles of the pharynx, muscles of larynx, sternocleidomastoid, and trapezius muscle. Tongue muscles receive fibers from the hypoglossal nerve. The corticobulbar tract indirectly affects muscles supplied by third, fourth, and sixth cranial nerve through medial longitudinal fasciculus and paramedian part of the pontine formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: All the muscles of the pharynx receive innervation from the tenth cranial nerve (vagus nerve) except the stylopharyngeus, which is innervated by the ninth cranial nerve (glossopharyngeal nerve). From the nucleus ambiguous that lies in the floor of the fourth ventricle, motor  fibers travel in the glossopharyngeal nerve and innervate the muscle. After its exit from the cranial cavity, the glossopharyngeal nerve descends in the neck, running anterolaterally to the internal carotid artery. On reaching the inferior margin of the stylopharyngeus muscle, the nerve gives off several branches that provide motor innervations to this muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Tongue Innervation
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve (cranial nerve XII) provides motor innervation of the tongue. This nerve arises from the hypoglossal nucleus and exits the skull through the hypoglossal canal. Upon reaching the neck, it crosses anterior to the internal and external carotid arteries and is typically located deep to the posterior belly of the digastric muscle. It can often be found below the digastric muscle as it moves anteriorly, making it susceptible to injury during dissection of neck levels 1B and 2A. The nerve then proceeds superomedially, deep to the mylohyoid muscle, to innervate both the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The palatoglossus muscle receives innervation from the pharyngeal branch of the cranial nerve X (vagus nerve). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 9 (Glossopharyngeal) -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: As stated above, the glossopharyngeal nerve provides motor innervation to the stylopharyngeus muscle, which elevates the pharynx and larynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The genioglossus muscle is under the control of multiple reflexes evoked by mechanoreceptors involved in the upper respiratory tract airway. [7] The tongue has five different means of innervation all of which include; the trigeminal nerve specifically the lingual nerve, the facial nerve specifically the chorda tympani branch, the glossopharyngeal nerve, and the hypoglossal nerve. [7] [8] The vagus nerve is involved in the innervation of the genioglossus.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Nucleus Ambiguus -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The nucleus ambiguus provides innervation to the muscles of the soft palate, larynx, and pharynx by contributing motor fibers to three cranial nerves: glossopharyngeal (CN IX), vagus (CN X), and spinal accessory (CN XI)  nerves. Axons leaving the nucleus ambiguus exit the medulla dorsally to the inferior olive to join fibers with the three cranial nerves. These three cranial nerves innervate the striated muscles of the soft palate, larynx, pharynx, and upper part of the esophagus. The glossopharyngeal nerve innervates the stylopharyngeus muscle; the vagus nerve innervates palatal muscles, pharyngeal and laryngeal muscles, and striated muscle of esophagus-palatoglossus; the spinal accessory nerve innervates the cranial portion of the laryngeal muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: There are four sets of paired extrinsic muscles: the styloglossus, hyoglossus, genioglossus, and palatoglossus muscles. The styloglossus muscle acts to lift the lateral edges and to retract the tongue. The hyoglossus muscle causes retraction as well as depression of the tongue. The genioglossus muscle protrudes the tongue. The palatoglossus muscles elevate the posterior part of the tongue. The former three muscles are innervated by the hypoglossal (XII) nerve, whereas the palatoglossus muscle is innervated by the vagus (X) nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Hyoglossus Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The extrinsic muscles of the tongue all originate outside of the tongue and then insert onto the tongue. There are 4 extrinsic muscles, genioglossus, hyoglossus, stylogossus, and palatoglossus. The hyoglossus muscle depresses and retracts the tongue. The styloglossus also acts to retract the tongue as well as elevating the sides of the tongue. The genioglossus muscle mainly acts to protrude the tongue but can also depress the tongue. The palatoglossus muscle elevates the posterior part of the tongue and is the only extrinsic muscle innervated by the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) instead of the hypoglossal nerve. It is also important to note the chondroglossus, which acts to depress the lingual root. The chondroglossus is most commonly described as part of the hyoglossus muscle; however, some argue that it is an extrinsic tongue muscle in its own regard as it differs from the hyoglossus in its origin and course. [1] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Skull -- Muscles -- Hypoglossal Nerve (CN XII)
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) mainly innervates the intrinsic muscles of the tongue. Its motor component also innervates the hyoglossus, genioglossus, and styloglossus muscles. A branch of C1 travels with CN XII to innervate the geniohyoid muscle. Of note, the palatoglossus muscle, despite its action to elevate the tongue, is innervated by CN X.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Skull -- Muscles -- Glossopharyngeal Nerve (CN IX)
retrieved_docs.content: The stylopharyngeus muscle, which raises and dilates the pharynx, is innervated by CN IX.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 9 (Glossopharyngeal) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The glossopharyngeal nerve carries sensory, efferent motor, and parasympathetic fibers. Its branches consist of tympanic, tonsillar, stylopharyngeal, carotid sinus nerve, branches to the tongue, lingual branches, and a communicating branch to cranial nerve X (vagus nerve). See Image. The Glossopharyngeal Nerve. Special visceral efferent fibers (branchial motor) are the main motor fibers of the glossopharyngeal nerve and supply motor innervation to the stylopharyngeus muscle. This muscle elevates the larynx and pharynx, especially during speaking and swallowing. General visceral efferent fibers (visceral motor) provide parasympathetic innervation to the parotid glands. The fibers originate in the inferior salivary nucleus, travel with the tympanic nerve through the foramen ovale, and synapse at the otic ganglion. General visceral afferent fibers (visceral sensory) carry sensory information from the carotid sinus and body. General somatic afferent fibers (general sensory) provide sensory innervation to the upper pharynx, the inner surface of the tympanic membrane, and the posterior third of the tongue. Special sensory fibers also provide taste afferents from this portion of the tongue. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Although primarily known as the motor nerve of the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, the accessory nerve (11th cranial nerve or cranial nerve XI) also contributes some fibers to the vagus nerve and indirectly influences pharyngeal muscular function. Sympathetic and parasympathetic autonomic nerves also contribute to the pharyngeal plexus. These nerves regulate the vascular and glandular elements of the pharynx but do not contribute to pharyngeal muscular function.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The extrinsic muscles of the larynx are found in bilateral pairs and contribute to the gross movement of the larynx. The innervation of these muscles varies, involving the ansa cervicalis, trigeminal nerve, facial nerve, glossopharyngeal nerve, and hypoglossal nerve. The pharyngeal constrictors and palatopharyngeus receive innervation from the glossopharyngeal, vagus, and spinal accessory nerves through the pharyngeal plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 12 (Hypoglossal) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The muscular/lingual branch is a general somatic efferent (GSE). It innervates all the tongue muscles (intrinsic and extrinsic) except for the palatoglossus, which receives motor innervation from the vagal nerve (CN X). The muscular branch controls most tongue actions, including protruding, retracting, depressing the tongue, and changing the tongue’s shape (see Image . Arteries and Veins of the Tongue). [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve also referred to as cranial nerve XII, is responsible for the innervation of both the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles; this includes the genioglossus muscles, as they are part of the extrinsic muscle group. Pleural receptors, when called upon (during an inhalation), send inhibitory afferents to the XII nucleus to make the genioglossus muscle more able to retract and better dilate the upper airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 176
retrieved_docs.content: parent term for “Migraine and Headache Syndromes,” and is clinically similar to and possibly synonymous with vascular headache (صداع وعائي), reflecting overlapping vascular and neural mechanisms. The nervous system also encompasses procedures such as muscle‑nerve entrapment (إحصار عصبي عضلي) and is closely linked to neurosurgical interventions for CSF diversion. Insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts—including the intraperitoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) and shunts to the nasopharynx—are essential for managing ventricular enlargement or hydrocephalus. Systemic nervous system disorders are broadly related to vascular headache, illustrating how anatomical sites, disease classifications, clinical similarities, and surgical treatments are interwoven in the comprehensive understanding and care of nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: concepts (pain, paralysis) with anatomical sites (peripheral nerves, specific cranial and spinal nerves, tendon sheaths, synovium), disease entities (headache, ocular disorders, the extensive list of neuropathies, muscular disorders, entrapment syndromes, rare hereditary and inflammatory conditions), and procedural interventions (nerve incision, decompression), providing clinicians with a comprehensive taxonomy for precise diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management of nervous‑system and sense‑organ disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of nerve is classified as a type of peripheral nerve decompression, indicating that it falls under the broader category of decompression procedures targeting peripheral nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.
